TextENGNew: In Hinamizawa, where the people believe that the blood of demons flows through their veins, the character for@bdemon.@< 鬼 @>is treated as deeply sacred.
For instance, the use of the character 鬼 in personal names was apparently a privilege reserved solely for the heads of the Kimiyoshi and Sonozaki families.
For example, in the name of the current Sonozaki head, Oryou -- written with the character 魎 -- one can clearly see the presence of 鬼.
This alone serves as proof of her legitimacy as head of the Sonozaki family.
The same applies to her heir, Mion Sonozaki,@kin whose name the character 魅 likewise contains 鬼.

Even Oryou's own daughter and Mion's mother, Akane -- who was unable to succeed as head -- @kwas known to have borne the name 蒐 before her disownment, though it was later changed to 茜 (both read as "Akane").
Incidentally, regarding the village chief's name "Kiichirou" -- the "ki" is thought to have originally carried the meaning of 鬼, or "demon."
Furthermore, names incorporating 鬼 are not limited to the Sonozaki family's heirs alone.
The character can also be found hidden within the surnames of the Three Families themselves.
For example, the name Kimiyoshi -- written 公由 -- was likely created by splitting the single character 鬼 apart. If you write 由 above 公, the character 鬼 emerges remarkably clearly.

As for the@b古手.@<Furude@> family, who have served as Shinto priests for generations -- the word for "fortune-teller" can be written as 占手.@kBy adding a demon's horn -- the stroke atop the character 鬼 -- the 占 becomes 古, giving us "Furude."
In the case of the Sonozaki family, perhaps because 鬼 is already embedded in their given names, the character does not appear in their surname.
As the clan responsible for overseeing the Watanagashi ceremony, it seems their surname was derived directly from the ceremony's content.

The "zaki" in Sonozaki is an alternate reading of "saki," meaning "to tear" or "to rend," while "sono" -- written 園 -- with its enclosed and complex structure, likely alludes to a living body, a creature.
changeReason: Old translation renders 生物 as 'human body' when it means 'living thing/creature'; new translation correctly uses 'a living body, a creature', matching the Japanese.

TextENGNew: In other words, it is thought that the name "Sonozaki" was chosen to covertly embody that meaning -- "those who rend open the body."
changeReason: Old translation specifies 'gut' while the Japanese implies the body/living creature more broadly (園 = 生物); new uses 'body'. Also 'rend' better matches 裂き than 'slice'.
